Classical testing theory: Fisher and Neyman-Pearson approaches.

Preliminaries

Let us start with a quick review of what a test is. In the common view, an hypothesisis to be tested is defined relative of its complementary hypothesis: if the data suggest it is false, what should we believe to be true?

Def: The two complementary hypotheses are called null hypothesis (𝐻0H0) and alternative hypothesis (𝐻1H1)

The Null Hypothesis is the hypothesis that is held to be true unless sufficient evidence to the contrary is obtained

In hypothesis testing, the objective is to use data to decide if 𝐻0H0 is plausible, or if the evidence does not support it enough and we need to reject it (although it is ofter true that we are actually hoping to reject it).

The definition of a hypothesis test requires the definition of a model (parametric or not) and a specification of the null and alternative hypotheses as submodels.

Parametric model

For the moment, we consider the case of a parametric model:

{𝑓(⋅∣𝜃):𝜃∈Θ}{f(⋅∣θ):θ∈Θ}
where 𝜃θ is a (possibly vector values) parameter, ΘΘ is the parameter space, 𝑓(⋅∣𝜃)f(⋅∣θ) is a family of distributions (continuous or discrete, univariate or not), indexed by 𝜃θ.

In this context, a hypothesis can take the form

𝐻0:𝜃∈Θ0𝑣𝑠𝐻1:𝜃∈Θ1,H0:θ∈Θ0vsH1:θ∈Θ1,
where typically (but not necessarily) Θ1=Θ∖Θ0Θ1=Θ∖Θ0 is the conjugate of the null parametric set.

We can have simple and composite hypotheses:

  • A simple hypothesis determines completely the distribution of 𝑋X, specifies only one possible distribution for the sample (in a parametric framework, it specifies exactly the parameter value). (for example given a Gaussian model with 𝜎2=1σ2=1, the hypothesis, 𝐻0:𝜇=0H0:μ=0)
  • a composite hypothesis specifies more that one possible distributions for the sample (for example, given a Gaussian model with 𝜎2=1σ2=1, the hypothesis 𝐻0:𝜇≥2H0:μ≥2)

Rejection region and error types

With this setting, we can define:

Def: A hypothesis procedure or hypothesis test is a rule that specifies:

  • For which sample values the decision is made to accept 𝐻0H0 as true
  • For which sample values 𝐻0H0 is rejected (and 𝐻1H1 is accepted as true)

The decision is based (typically) on the definition of a rejection region (or critical region) defined by the subset of the sample space that leads to reject the null hypothesis.

The rejection region 𝑅R of a hypothesis test is usually defined through a test statistic 𝑊(𝑋)W(X), a function of the sample

𝑅={𝐗:𝑊(𝐗)>𝑐}⇒𝑟𝑒𝑗𝑒𝑐𝑡𝐻0R={X:W(X)>c}⇒rejectH0
𝐴={𝐗:𝑊(𝐗)≤𝑐}⇒𝑎𝑐𝑐𝑒𝑝𝑡𝐻0A={X:W(X)≤c}⇒acceptH0
 The definition of 𝑅R as a function of 𝑊W directly simplifies the definition of 𝑅R, because permits to write it as a subset of ℝR instead of ℝ𝑛Rn
𝑅𝑤={𝑤:𝑊>𝑐}Rw={w:W>c}
 Once the region is defined, and a decision (reject or not) is taken, there is no guarantee that it is correct.

Errors

In fact, there are 4 possible outcomes of the decision problem, two of which correspond to wrong decisions: a type I error occurs if 𝐻0H0 is rejected when it is actually true; a type II error occurs if 𝐻0H0 is not rejected when it is actually false.

Decision H0 is true H1 is true
H0 Correct (1−𝛼1−α) Type II error
H1 Type I error (𝛼α) Correct

Power function

Errors in hypothesis testing are not symmetric. Conventionally, the statistical testing procedures (both Fisher and Neyman-Pearson) tend to assign a greater importance to 𝛼α, the type I error. This is the error that is controlled for. This is the reason why the null hypothesis is (quite often) defined as the one whose incorrect rejection is considered more severe.

Unfortunately, the two error types enter into conflict: by reducing 𝛼α, the type II error increases, and vice-versa.

Example: trade-off between Type I and Type II errors

library(shiny)
library(dplyr)

To keep things simple, we assume the same Gaussian population 𝑋∼𝑁(𝜇,𝜎2)X∼N(μ,σ2), 𝜎2=1σ2=1 known and the following simple hypotheses:

𝐻0:𝜇=0𝑣𝑠𝐻1:𝜇=2H0:μ=0vsH1:μ=2

The test statistic used for this type of hypotheses is

𝑍=𝑋¯𝜎/𝑛√Z=X¯σ/n
The rejection region is 𝑍>𝑡(𝑛−1)1−𝛼/2Z>t1−α/2(n−1).

0.0010.25000.050.0010.0260.0510.0760.1010.1260.1510.1760.2010.2260.25

The type I and type II errors can’t be both minimized.

type I error type II error
error probabilities 0.050 0.361

Power function and Neyman-Pearson theorem

We now need to recall some definitions

Def: A test procedure with rejection region 𝑅R, for the hypothesis

𝐻0:𝜃∈Θ0𝑣𝑠𝐻1:𝜃∈Θ1H0:θ∈Θ0vsH1:θ∈Θ1
has power function 𝛽(𝜃)β(θ) defined as:
𝛽(𝜃)=𝑃𝑟(𝐗∈𝑅∣𝜃)β(θ)=Pr(X∈R∣θ)
 for 𝜃∈Θ0∪Θ1θ∈Θ0∪Θ1.

So, the power is the probability to reject the null hypothesis given a certain value of 𝜃θ.

Let 𝑋1,…,𝑋10X1,…,X10 be a random sample from a population 𝑁(𝜇,𝜎2=4)N(μ,σ2=4) and let

𝐻0:𝜇≤0and 𝐻1:𝜇>0H0:μ≤0and H1:μ>0
and let consider a test with
𝑅={𝑋¯>1}R={X¯>1}
 Then,
𝛽(𝜇)=𝑃(𝑋¯>1∣𝜇=𝜇)=𝑃⎛⎝⎜⎜𝑍>1−𝜇210√⎞⎠⎟⎟β(μ)=P(X¯>1∣μ=μ)=P(Z>1−μ210)

Level and size of a test

Def:

  • For 0≤𝛼≤10≤α≤1, a test with power function 𝛽(𝜃)β(θ) is a size 𝛼α test if
    𝑠𝑢𝑝𝜃∈Θ0𝛽(𝜃)=𝛼supθ∈Θ0β(θ)=α
  • For 0≤𝛼≤10≤α≤1, a test with power function 𝛽(𝜃)β(θ) is a level 𝛼α test if
    𝑠𝑢𝑝𝜃∈Θ0𝛽(𝜃)≤𝛼supθ∈Θ0β(θ)≤α

Uniformly most powerful tests

Fisher advocated the use of a test statistic and look for evidence agaist the null through the significance level but was not so concerned about the definition of an optimality procedure that could be valid universally.

Neyman and Pearson proposed this resut because they wanted to give a mathematical justification to the tests proposed by Fisher’s procedure.

Since both type I and type II errors can’t be simultaneously minimized, Neyman and Pearson proposed to select the best test based on a constrained minimization problem: minimization of Type II error, given the constraint on a fixed Type I error (level) 𝛼α.

They elaborate the concept of Most powerful and Uniformly most powerful (UMP) tests.

Def: Let 𝒞C be a class of tests for testing 𝐻0:𝜃∈Θ0H0:θ∈Θ0 versus 𝐻1:𝜃∈Θ1H1:θ∈Θ1. A test belonging to a class 𝒞C (of possible tests), with power function 𝛽(𝜃)β(θ), is a uniformly most powerful (UMP) class 𝒞C test if, 𝛽(𝜃)≥𝛽′(𝜃)β(θ)≥β′(θ) for every 𝜃∈Θ1(=Θ𝑐0)θ∈Θ1(=Θ0c) and for every 𝛽′(𝜃)β′(θ) that is a power function of a test in class 𝒞C.

If the chosen family 𝒞C is the set of 𝛼α level tests, then the definition above gives the UMP level 𝛼α tests.

If Θ0={𝜃0}Θ0={θ0} and Θ1={𝜃1}Θ1={θ1} (simple hypotheses), then a test satisfying the definition above is said most powerful.

Neyman-Pearson Lemma

For technical reasons, it is convenient to allow a randomization mechanism, to help with the decision between 𝐻0H0 and 𝐻1H1.

A randomized test is characterized by a critical function 𝜙ϕ with range in [0,1][0,1]. Given 𝑥x, 𝜙(𝑥)ϕ(x) is the probability to reject 𝐻0H0.

In this case, the power function corresponds to the expectation of the critical function with respect to a given parameter:

𝛽(𝜃)=𝐸𝜃𝑃𝜃{reject 𝐻0∣𝑋}=E𝜃𝜙(𝑋)β(θ)=EθPθ{reject H0∣X}=Eθϕ(X)
Note that the set of critical functions ΦΦ is convex: if 𝜙1ϕ1 and 𝜙2ϕ2 are critical function (belong to ΦΦ), then for any 𝜏∈(0,1)τ∈(0,1), also 𝜏𝜙1+(1−𝜏)𝜙2∈Φτϕ1+(1−τ)ϕ2∈Φ.

A nonrandomized test is a randomized test where 𝑅={𝑥:𝜙(𝑥)=1}R={x:ϕ(x)=1}.

Theorem (Neyman-Pearson (1933)) Consider testing 𝐻0:𝜃=𝜃0H0:θ=θ0 versus 𝐻1:𝜃=𝜃1H1:θ=θ1 where the pdf or pmf corresponding to 𝜃𝑖θi is 𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃𝑖),𝑖=0,1f(x∣θi),i=0,1.

  1. There exist a test 𝜙ϕ and a constant 𝑘k such that
    𝐸𝜃0𝜙(𝑋)=𝛼andEθ0ϕ(X)=αand
    𝜙(𝑥)={10when 𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃1)>𝑘𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃0)when 𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃1)<𝑘𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃0)ϕ(x)={1when f(x∣θ1)>kf(x∣θ0)0when f(x∣θ1)<kf(x∣θ0)
  2. If a test satisfies the above conditions for some 𝑘k, then it is most powerful for testing 𝜃0θ0 against 𝜃1θ1
  3. If 𝜙ϕ is most powerful, then there exists 𝑘k such that 𝜙ϕ is defined as above (a.e.) unless there exists a test with size <𝛼<α and power 1.

Remark If 𝜙∈{0,1}ϕ∈{0,1} (nonrandomized test), the Neyman-Pearson Lemma states that every most powerful level 𝛼α test, if it exists, must have the rejection region:

𝑅={𝑥:𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃1)≥𝑘𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃0)}R={x:f(x∣θ1)≥kf(x∣θ0)}

for some 𝑘>0k>0 for which 𝑃𝜃0(𝑋∈𝑅)=𝛼Pθ0(X∈R)=α.

Also, if a test with rejection region 𝑅R as above exists and 𝑃𝜃0(𝑋∈𝑅)=𝛼Pθ0(X∈R)=α exists, then the UMP 𝛼−α−level test is a size 𝛼α test.

Proof 1- Let 𝛼(𝑘)=𝑃𝜃0{𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃1)>𝑘𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃0)}α(k)=Pθ0{f(x∣θ1)>kf(x∣θ0)}. Assume 𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃0)>0f(x∣θ0)>0 (the probability is computed under 𝐻0H0). 𝛼(𝑘)α(k) is the probability that the rv 𝑓(𝑋∣𝜃1)/𝑓(𝑋∣𝜃0)>𝑘f(X∣θ1)/f(X∣θ0)>k. Thus, 1−𝛼(𝑘)1−α(k) is a CDF (not necessarily continuous), with 𝛼(𝑘−)−𝛼(𝑘)=𝑃𝜃0(𝑓(𝑋∣𝜃1)/𝑓(𝑋∣𝜃0)=𝑘)α(k−)−α(k)=Pθ0(f(X∣θ1)/f(X∣θ0)=k).

Given 𝛼∈(0,1)α∈(0,1), let 𝑘0k0 be such that 𝛼(𝑘0)≤𝛼≤𝛼(𝑘−0)α(k0)≤α≤α(k0−) and define

𝜙(𝑥)=⎧⎩⎨⎪⎪1𝛼−𝛼(𝑘0)𝛼(𝑘−0)−𝛼(𝑘0)0when 𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃1)>𝑘0𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃0)when 𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃1)=𝑘0𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃0)when 𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃1)<𝑘0𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃0)ϕ(x)={1when f(x∣θ1)>k0f(x∣θ0)α−α(k0)α(k0−)−α(k0)when f(x∣θ1)=k0f(x∣θ0)0when f(x∣θ1)<k0f(x∣θ0)

If 𝛼(𝑘−0)−𝛼(𝑘0)=0α(k0−)−α(k0)=0, then 𝑃𝜃0{𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃1)=𝑘0𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃0)}=0Pθ0{f(x∣θ1)=k0f(x∣θ0)}=0 and 𝜙ϕ is defined a.e.

It is easy to see that the size of the test is 𝐸𝜃0𝜙(𝑋)=𝛼Eθ0ϕ(X)=α

2- Suppose that 𝜙ϕ satisfies the definition of the theorem, and assume 𝜙∗ϕ∗ is any other test with 𝐸𝜃0𝜙∗≤𝛼Eθ0ϕ∗≤α.

Let

𝑆+={𝑥:𝜙(𝑥)−𝜙∗(𝑥)>0}and𝑆−={𝜙(𝑥)−𝜙∗(𝑥)<0}S+={x:ϕ(x)−ϕ∗(x)>0}andS−={ϕ(x)−ϕ∗(x)<0}

If 𝑥∈𝑆+x∈S+, then necessarily 𝜙(𝑥)>0ϕ(x)>0 and thus 𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃1)≥𝑘𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃0)f(x∣θ1)≥kf(x∣θ0). If 𝑥∈𝑆−x∈S−, then necessarily 𝜙(𝑥)<1ϕ(x)<1 and thus 𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃1)≤𝑘𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃0)f(x∣θ1)≤kf(x∣θ0).

Thus,

∫(𝜙(𝑥)−𝜙∗(𝑥))(𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃1)−𝑘𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃0))𝑑𝑥=∫𝑆+∪𝑆−(𝜙(𝑥)−𝜙∗(𝑥))(𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃1)−𝑘𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃0))𝑑𝑥≥0∫(ϕ(x)−ϕ∗(x))(f(x∣θ1)−kf(x∣θ0))dx=∫S+∪S−(ϕ(x)−ϕ∗(x))(f(x∣θ1)−kf(x∣θ0))dx≥0
and this implies
𝛽𝜙(𝜃1)−𝛽𝜙∗(𝜃1)=𝐸𝜃1𝜙(𝑋)−𝐸𝜃1𝜙∗(𝑋)=∫(𝜙(𝑥)−𝜙∗(𝑥))𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃1)𝑑𝑥≥∫(𝜙(𝑥)−𝜙∗(𝑥))𝑘𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃0)𝑑𝑥≥0βϕ(θ1)−βϕ∗(θ1)=Eθ1ϕ(X)−Eθ1ϕ∗(X)=∫(ϕ(x)−ϕ∗(x))f(x∣θ1)dx≥∫(ϕ(x)−ϕ∗(x))kf(x∣θ0)dx≥0

3- See (Lehmann and Romano 2022)

Example Suppose 𝑋∼𝑏𝑖𝑛𝑜𝑚𝑖𝑎𝑙(2,𝜃)X∼binomial(2,θ) and we are interested in testing 𝐻0:𝜃=1/2H0:θ=1/2 vs 𝐻1:𝜃=3/4H1:θ=3/4.

The likelihood ratio

𝐿(𝑋)=𝑓3/4(𝑋)𝑓1/2(𝑋)=(2𝑋)(3/4)𝑋(1/4)2−𝑋(2𝑋)(1/2)2=3𝑋4=(under 𝐻0)⎧⎩⎨⎪⎪1/43/49/4𝑝𝑟.1/4𝑝𝑟.1/2𝑝𝑟.1/4L(X)=f3/4(X)f1/2(X)=(2X)(3/4)X(1/4)2−X(2X)(1/2)2=3X4=(under H0){1/4pr.1/43/4pr.1/29/4pr.1/4

Assume 𝛼=0.05α=0.05 is the desired level.

We have 𝑃𝜃0(𝐿(𝑋)≤3/4)=3/4<1−𝛼Pθ0(L(X)≤3/4)=3/4<1−α, thus 𝑘>3/4k>3/4, but if 𝑘<9/4k<9/4, then 𝑃𝜃0(𝐿(𝑋)>𝑘)=𝑃𝜃0(𝐿(𝑋)=9/4)=1/4>𝛼Pθ0(L(X)>k)=Pθ0(L(X)=9/4)=1/4>α, thus 𝑘≥9/4k≥9/4.

However, 𝑘>9/4k>9/4 is also impossible, because then 𝑃𝜃0(𝐿(𝑋)>𝑘)=0Pθ0(L(X)>k)=0 (implying 𝜙=0ϕ=0).

Thus, we must have 𝑘=9/4k=9/4 and 𝜙(0)=𝜙(1)=0ϕ(0)=ϕ(1)=0, while 𝜙(2)ϕ(2) is determined by:

𝛼=𝐸𝜃0𝜙(𝑋)=14𝜙(2)⇒𝜙(2)=4𝛼.α=Eθ0ϕ(X)=14ϕ(2)⇒ϕ(2)=4α.

From factorization theorem, it is easy to derive:

Corollary Consider testing 𝐻0:𝜃=𝜃0H0:θ=θ0 versus 𝐻1:𝜃=𝜃1H1:θ=θ1. Suppose 𝑇(𝑋)T(X) is a sufficient statistics for 𝜃θ and 𝑔(𝑡|𝜃𝑖),𝑖=0,1g(t|θi),i=0,1 is its prob. density function corresponding to 𝜃𝑖θi. Then any test statistics based on 𝑇T with rejection region 𝑆S is a most powerful level 𝛼α test if it satisfies

𝑡=𝑇(𝑥)∈𝑆𝑖𝑓𝑔(𝑡|𝜃1)>𝑘𝑔(𝑡|𝜃0)t=T(x)∈Sifg(t|θ1)>kg(t|θ0)
and
𝑡=𝑇(𝑥)∈𝑆𝐶𝑖𝑓𝑔(𝑡|𝜃1)<𝑘𝑔(𝑡|𝜃0)t=T(x)∈SCifg(t|θ1)<kg(t|θ0)
 and
𝛼=𝑃𝜃0(𝑇∈𝑆)α=Pθ0(T∈S)

Example 𝑋∼𝑒𝑥𝑝𝑜𝑛𝑒𝑛𝑡𝑖𝑎𝑙(𝜆)X∼exponential(λ) and

𝐻0:𝜆=1𝑣𝑠𝐻1:𝜆=2H0:λ=1vsH1:λ=2
The rejection region must be defined as:
𝑅={𝑥:𝑘𝑒−∑𝑖𝑥𝑖<2𝑛𝑒−2∑𝑖𝑥𝑖}R={x:ke−∑ixi<2ne−2∑ixi}
 where 𝑘k is such that
𝑃(𝑋∈𝑅∣𝜆=1)=𝑃1(𝑘𝑒−∑𝑖𝑋𝑖<2𝑛𝑒−2∑𝑖𝑋𝑖)=𝑃1(𝑒∑𝑖𝑋𝑖<2𝑛/𝑘)=𝑃1(∑𝑖𝑋𝑖<𝑛log2−log(𝑘))=𝛼P(X∈R∣λ=1)=P1(ke−∑iXi<2ne−2∑iXi)=P1(e∑iXi<2n/k)=P1(∑iXi<nlog⁡2−log⁡(k))=α

Since ∑𝑖𝑋𝑖∼𝐺𝑎𝑚𝑚𝑎(𝑛,1)∑iXi∼Gamma(n,1), one can find 𝑘k.

Likelihood ratio test

According to N-P Lemma, test of the form:

𝑅={𝑥:𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃1)𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃0)≥𝑘}R={x:f(x∣θ1)f(x∣θ0)≥k}
is an MP test of level 𝛼=𝑃𝜃0(𝑋∈𝑅)α=Pθ0(X∈R) (unless the event {𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃1)𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃0)=𝑘}{f(x∣θ1)f(x∣θ0)=k} has positive probabilty, in that case randomization is necessary).

The region 𝑅R is defined as the ratio between the likelihood function under the alternative and the likelihood function under the null hypothesis.

This is very simple because NP Lemma only considers simple hypothesis (both for 𝐻0H0 and 𝐻1H1).

This suggests the definition of likelihood ratio tests for arbitrary testing problems.

Generalized likelihood ratio tests

Suppose we have

𝐻0:𝜃∈Θ0𝑣𝑠𝐻1:𝜃∈Θ𝑐𝑜H0:θ∈Θ0vsH1:θ∈Θoc
The values fof 𝜃θ under 𝐻0H0 and 𝐻1H1 are not unique

However, suppose that the MLE exists over the whole parameter space Θ=Θ0∪Θ𝑐0Θ=Θ0∪Θ0c

We can also obtain the MLE for 𝜃θ under the null hypothesis (through constrained optimization):

𝜃̂ 0=argmax𝜃∈Θ0ℓ(𝜃∣𝑥)=argmax𝜃∈Θ0log𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃)θ^0=arg⁡maxθ∈Θ0ℓ(θ∣x)=arg⁡maxθ∈Θ0log⁡f(x∣θ)
Then, we take the ratio between the likelihood under 𝐻0H0, 𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃̂ 0)f(x∣θ^0) and the likelihood under the parameter space ΘΘ (that is the unconstrained MLE):
𝜃̂ =argmax𝜃∈Θℓ(𝜃∣𝑥)θ^=arg⁡maxθ∈Θℓ(θ∣x)

The (generalized) likelihood ratio test statistic is then:

𝜆(𝑥)=max𝜃∈Θ𝐿(𝜃∣𝑥)max𝜃∈Θ0𝐿(𝜃∣𝑥)=𝐿(𝜃̂ ∣𝑥)𝐿(𝜃0^∣𝑥)=𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃̂ )𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃̂ 0)λ(x)=maxθ∈ΘL(θ∣x)maxθ∈Θ0L(θ∣x)=L(θ^∣x)L(θ0^∣x)=f(x∣θ^)f(x∣θ^0)

  • In typical situations, only one of the optimizations is constrained

  • The numerator is always larger than the denominator (only equal to 1 if 𝜃̂ 0=𝜃̂ θ^0=θ^)

  • The smaller the ratio, the larger the evidence in favor of the null

  • The larger the ratio, the larger the evidence against the null

The likelihood ratio test (LRT) is any test that has a rejection region of the form : {𝑥:𝜆(𝑥)≥𝑐}{x:λ(x)≥c} where 𝑐c is any number satisfying 𝑐>1c>1.

Monotone likelihood ratio

To generalize the optimality result of Neyman and Pearson to non-simple hypotheses, we need to introduce the following:

Def: A family of pdfs {𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃);𝜃∈Θ}{f(x∣θ);θ∈Θ} has monotone likelihood ratio if there exists a function 𝑇(𝑥)T(x) such that whenever 𝜃2>𝜃1θ2>θ1, the ratio

𝑓(𝑡∣𝜃2)𝑓(𝑡∣𝜃1)f(t∣θ2)f(t∣θ1)
is a nondecreasing function of 𝑡=𝑇(𝑥)t=T(x) on
𝓍={𝑡:𝑓(𝑡∣𝜃1)>0or𝑓(𝑡∣𝜃2)>0}.x={t:f(t∣θ1)>0orf(t∣θ2)>0}.
 Further, the distribution is identifiable, that is 𝑔(𝑡;𝜃1)≠𝑔(𝑡;𝜃2)g(t;θ1)≠g(t;θ2) whenever 𝜃1≠𝜃2θ1≠θ2

Example The exponential family

𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃)=exp{𝑤(𝜃)𝑡(𝑥)−𝑏(𝜃)}ℎ(𝑥)f(x∣θ)=exp⁡{w(θ)t(x)−b(θ)}h(x)
if 𝑤(𝜃)w(θ) strictly increasing has likelihood ratio monotone in 𝑇=𝑡(𝑥)T=t(x).

Example Another example of MLR distributions are location families with densities 𝑔(𝑥−𝜃)g(x−θ) for which −𝑙𝑜𝑔𝑔−logg is convex. (See example 1 Ch.9 Lehmann (1986))

Karlin and Rubin theorem for one-sided tests

Consider the one-sided composite test

𝐻0:𝜃≤𝜃0vs𝐻1:𝜃>𝜃0H0:θ≤θ0vsH1:θ>θ0

Theorem (Karlin and Rubin) Suppose that 𝑋X has density 𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃)f(x∣θ) with monotone likelihood ratio in 𝑇(𝑥)T(x). Then:

  1. The test 𝜙∗ϕ∗ defined by

    𝜙∗(𝑥)=⎧⎩⎨⎪⎪1𝛾0𝑇(𝑥)>𝑐𝑇(𝑥)=𝑐𝑇(𝑥)<𝑐ϕ∗(x)={1T(x)>cγT(x)=c0T(x)<c
    is a uniformly most powerful (UMP) level 𝛼α test where 𝛼=𝐸𝜃0𝜙∗α=Eθ0ϕ∗.

    The constants 𝑐c and 𝛾γ can be adjusted to attain the desired significance level.

  2. The power function of 𝜙∗ϕ∗ is nondecreasing, strictly increasing if 0<𝛽(𝜃)<10<β(θ)<1

Proof

Power is nondecreasing. Suppose 𝜃1<𝜃2θ1<θ2 and set

𝐿(𝑥)=𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃2)𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃1)=𝑔(𝑇(𝑥)∣𝜃2)𝑔(𝑇(𝑥)∣𝜃1)L(x)=f(x∣θ2)f(x∣θ1)=g(T(x)∣θ2)g(T(x)∣θ1)
By assumption, 𝐿L is a nondecreasing function of 𝑇(𝑥)T(x). Let 𝑘k be such that (𝑇=𝑐)↦(𝐿=𝑘)(T=c)↦(L=k). Then
𝜙∗(𝑥)={10𝐿>𝑘𝐿<𝑘ϕ∗(x)={1L>k0L<k
 is a likelihood ratio test for the simple hypothesis 𝐻0:𝜃=𝜃1H0:θ=θ1 vs 𝐻1:𝜃=𝜃2H1:θ=θ2. By the corollary to the N-P Lemma, this test is most powerful and thus (see Corollary 12.4. (Keener 2010)) 𝐸𝜃1𝜙∗≤𝐸𝜃2𝜙∗Eθ1ϕ∗≤Eθ2ϕ∗ (with strict inequality unless both expetations are either 0 or 1).

So, 𝜙∗ϕ∗ has level

sup𝜃≤𝜃0𝐸𝜃𝜙∗(𝑋)=𝐸𝜃0𝜙∗(𝑋)=𝛼supθ≤θ0Eθϕ∗(X)=Eθ0ϕ∗(X)=α
and nondecreasing power as stated in 2.

𝜙∗ϕ∗ is UMP

It remains to show that any test 𝜙̃ ϕ~ such that 𝐸𝜃0𝜙̃ (𝑋)≤𝛼Eθ0ϕ~(X)≤α has lower power than 𝜙∗ϕ∗.

Take an arbitrary 𝜃1>𝜃0θ1>θ0. From the NP Lemma, 𝜙∗ϕ∗ is the most powerful test for the simple hypothesis 𝐻0:𝜃=𝜃0H0:θ=θ0 against the alternative 𝐻1:𝜃=𝜃1H1:θ=θ1, thus implying 𝐸𝜃1𝜙∗≥𝐸𝜃1𝜙̃ Eθ1ϕ∗≥Eθ1ϕ~

Choice of 𝑐c and 𝛾γ

Finally, we show that 𝑐c nd 𝛾γ can be chosen so that 𝐸𝜃0𝜙∗=𝛼Eθ0ϕ∗=α. Let 𝐺0G0 be the CDF of 𝑇(𝑋)T(X) when 𝜃=𝜃0θ=θ0. Let 𝑐=sup{𝑡:𝐺0(𝑡)≤1−𝛼}c=sup{t:G0(t)≤1−α}.

  • If 𝑡=𝑇(𝑥)>𝑐t=T(x)>c, then 𝐺0(𝑡)>1−𝛼G0(t)>1−α.

  • 𝐺0G0 is right continuous, so 𝐺0(𝑐)=lim𝑡↓𝑐𝐺0(𝑡)≥1−𝛼G0(c)=limt↓cG0(t)≥1−α

  • For 𝑡<𝑐t<c, 𝐺0(𝑡)≤1−𝛼G0(t)≤1−α and 𝐺0(𝑐−)=lim𝑡↑𝑐𝐺0(𝑡)≤1−𝛼G0(c−)=limt↑cG0(t)≤1−α

  • Define

    𝛾=𝐺0(𝑐)−(1−𝛼)𝐺0(𝑐)−𝐺0(𝑐−)=𝐺0(𝑐)−(1−𝛼)𝑃𝜃0(𝑇=𝑐)γ=G0(c)−(1−α)G0(c)−G0(c−)=G0(c)−(1−α)Pθ0(T=c)
    (note that if 𝐺0(𝑐)−𝐺0(𝑐−)=0G0(c)−G0(c−)=0, then 𝛾γ need not to be defined).

  • Finally,

    𝐸𝜃0𝜙∗=𝛾𝑃𝜃0(𝑇=𝑐)+1⋅𝑃𝜃0(𝑇>𝑐)=𝐺0(𝑐)−(1−𝛼)+(1−𝐺0(𝑐))=𝛼Eθ0ϕ∗=γPθ0(T=c)+1⋅Pθ0(T>c)=G0(c)−(1−α)+(1−G0(c))=α

Two-sided hypotheses

This example is to show that there can’t be a UMP test in the case of two-sided alternatives.

Consider the hypothesis

𝐻0:𝜃=𝜃0 vs 𝐻1:𝜃≠𝜃0,H0:θ=θ0 vs H1:θ≠θ0,
where 𝜃θ is the population mean from a Gaussian family with known variance 𝜎2σ2.

  • Suppose we define a test with 𝑅1(𝑋)={𝑇(𝑋)≤𝑡0}R1(X)={T(X)≤t0}
    𝛼=𝑃(𝑋¯≤𝑡0∣𝜃0)=𝑃(𝑋¯−𝜃0𝜎/𝑛√≤𝑡0−𝜃0𝜎/𝑛√)α=P(X¯≤t0∣θ0)=P(X¯−θ0σ/n≤t0−θ0σ/n)
     Then, 𝑡0−𝜃0𝜎/𝑛√=𝑧𝛼⇒𝑡0=𝜃0+𝑧𝛼𝜎𝑛√=𝜃0−𝑧1−𝛼𝜎𝑛√t0−θ0σ/n=zα⇒t0=θ0+zασn=θ0−z1−ασn

And, by Karlin-Rubin, this test is UMP for

𝐻0:𝜃=𝜃0𝑣𝑠𝐻1:𝜃<𝜃0.H0:θ=θ0vsH1:θ<θ0.

  • Repeat the same reasoning with 𝑅2(𝑋)={𝑇(𝑋)≥𝑡0}R2(X)={T(X)≥t0}, we get

    𝛼=𝑃(𝑋¯≥𝑡0∣𝜃0)=𝑃(𝑋¯−𝜃0𝜎/𝑛√≥𝑡0−𝜃0𝜎/𝑛√)α=P(X¯≥t0∣θ0)=P(X¯−θ0σ/n≥t0−θ0σ/n)
    Now 𝑡0=𝜃0+𝑧1−𝛼𝜎𝑛√=𝜃0−𝑧𝛼𝜎𝑛√t0=θ0+z1−ασn=θ0−zασn is UMP for
    𝐻0:𝜃=𝜃0𝑣𝑠𝐻1:𝜃>𝜃0.H0:θ=θ0vsH1:θ>θ0.

  • The power of the test based on 𝑅1R1 is

    𝛽1(𝜃)=𝑃𝜃(𝑋¯<𝜃0−𝑧1−𝛼𝜎𝑛√)=𝑃𝜃(𝑋¯−𝜃𝜎/𝑛√<𝜃0−𝜃𝜎/𝑛√−𝑧1−𝛼)β1(θ)=Pθ(X¯<θ0−z1−ασn)=Pθ(X¯−θσ/n<θ0−θσ/n−z1−α)

  • The power of the test based on 𝑅2R2 is

    𝛽2(𝜃)=𝑃𝜃(𝑋¯>𝜃0+𝑧1−𝛼𝜎𝑛√)=𝑃𝜃(𝑋¯−𝜃𝜎/𝑛√>𝜃0−𝜃𝜎/𝑛√+𝑧1−𝛼)β2(θ)=Pθ(X¯>θ0+z1−ασn)=Pθ(X¯−θσ/n>θ0−θσ/n+z1−α)

  • Let 𝜃′>𝜃0θ′>θ0, then

    𝛽1(𝜃′)=𝑃𝜃′(𝑋¯−𝜃′𝜎/𝑛√≤𝜃0−𝜃′𝜎/𝑛√−𝑧1−𝛼)=Φ(𝜃0−𝜃′𝜎/𝑛√−𝑧1−𝛼)<Φ(−𝑧1−𝛼)=1−Φ(𝑧1−𝛼)<1−Φ(𝜃0−𝜃′𝜎/𝑛√+𝑧1−𝛼)=𝛽2(𝜃′)β1(θ′)=Pθ′(X¯−θ′σ/n≤θ0−θ′σ/n−z1−α)=Φ(θ0−θ′σ/n−z1−α)<Φ(−z1−α)=1−Φ(z1−α)<1−Φ(θ0−θ′σ/n+z1−α)=β2(θ′)

  • Similarly, for 𝜃′<𝜃0θ′<θ0, 𝛽2(𝜃′)<𝛽1(𝜃′)β2(θ′)<β1(θ′), so there is no UMP

Unbiased and UMPU tests

  • The reason why in many cases we can’t find the UMP test is that the class 𝒞C of level-𝛼α tests is too large.

  • We can compromise by restricting the class of tests we consider to compare the power functions in the a subclass of unbiased test statistics

Def: A test for the hypothesis

𝜃∈Θ0𝑣𝑠𝜃∈Θ𝑐0θ∈Θ0vsθ∈Θ0c
with power function 𝛽(𝜃)β(θ) is called if
𝛽(𝜃′)≥𝛽(𝜃″)for all 𝜃′∈Θ𝑐0 and 𝜃″∈Θ0β(θ′)≥β(θ″)for all θ′∈Θ0c and θ″∈Θ0

  • An unbiased test must reject a false hypothesis (any 𝜃′∈Θ𝑐0θ′∈Θ0c) with a probability larger than the probability to reject a true hypothesis (𝜃″∈Θ0θ″∈Θ0).

  • If 𝛼=sup𝜃∈Θ0𝛽(𝜃)α=supθ∈Θ0β(θ) is the size of the unbiased test, then

    𝛽(𝜃)≥𝛼,for all 𝜃∈Θ𝑐0.β(θ)≥α,forall θ∈Θ0c.

  • A test that is uniformly most powerful among all umbiased tests is called an UMPU test

UMPU tests

  • UMPU tests for the two-sided hypothesis
    𝐻0:𝜃=𝜃0vs𝐻:1𝜃≠𝜃0H0:θ=θ0vsH:1θ≠θ0
     exist for exponential families.

Theorem Let 𝛼∈(0,1)α∈(0,1) and 𝜃0∈𝑖𝑛𝑡(Θ)θ0∈int(Θ). If 𝑋X has a distribution of the form

𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃)=𝑐(𝜃)ℎ(𝑥)exp{𝑡(𝑥)𝑤(𝜃)}f(x∣θ)=c(θ)h(x)exp⁡{t(x)w(θ)}
with ∞>𝑤′(𝜃0)>0∞>w′(θ0)>0. Then there exists a uniformly most powerful unbiased test 𝜙∗ϕ∗ of level 𝛼α, satisfying 𝛽′(𝜃0)=0β′(θ0)=0

  • 𝜙∗ϕ∗ is a two-sided test: namely takes the form
    𝜙={10(𝑇<𝑡𝑙)∪(𝑇>𝑡𝑢)𝑡𝑙<𝑇<𝑡𝑢ϕ={1(T<tl)∪(T>tu)0tl<T<tu
     and min{𝐸𝜙1{𝑇≤𝑡𝑙},𝐸𝜙1{𝑇≥𝑡𝑢}}>0min{Eϕ1{T≤tl},Eϕ1{T≥tu}}>0.

𝒞𝑚Cm tests

The previous theorem comes as a corollary case of the following more general result:

  • Let 𝜙+ϕ+ and 𝜙−ϕ− be thw UMP tests for the one-sided alternatives 𝜃>𝜃0θ>θ0 and 𝜃<𝜃0θ<θ0 with powers 𝛽+(𝜃)β+(θ) and 𝛽−(𝜃)β−(θ).

  • From UMP property of 𝜙+ϕ+ and 𝜙−ϕ− it is easy to see that for any test 𝜙ϕ,

    𝑚−=:𝛽′−(𝜃0)≤𝛽′𝜙(𝜃0)≤lim𝜀↓0𝛽+(𝜃0+𝜀)−𝛽+(𝜃0)𝜀=𝛽′+(𝜃0):=𝑚+m−=:β−′(θ0)≤βϕ′(θ0)≤limε↓0β+(θ0+ε)−β+(θ0)ε=β+′(θ0):=m+

  • Now, denote 𝒞𝑚Cm the class of level 𝛼α tests such that 𝛽′𝜙(𝜃0)=𝑚βϕ′(θ0)=m

  • Note that unbiased tests must have 𝑚=0m=0: suppose for example 𝑚<0m<0, then 𝛽′𝜙(𝜃0)=𝑚βϕ′(θ0)=m and 𝛽𝜙βϕ is decreasing at 𝜃0θ0. Thus, for 𝜃′>𝜃0θ′>θ0, we get 𝛽𝜙(𝜃′)<𝛽𝜙(𝜃0)βϕ(θ′)<βϕ(θ0), that contradicts unbiasedness, because 𝜃′∈Θ1θ′∈Θ1

UMP tests in 𝒞𝑚Cm

Theorem (th.12.22 in Keener, 2010) Let 𝛼∈(0,1)α∈(0,1) and 𝜃0∈𝑖𝑛𝑡(Θ)θ0∈int(Θ). If 𝑋X has a distribution of the form

𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃)=𝑐(𝜃)ℎ(𝑥)exp{𝑡(𝑥)𝑤(𝜃)}f(x∣θ)=c(θ)h(x)exp⁡{t(x)w(θ)}
with ∞>𝑤′(𝜃0)>0∞>w′(θ0)>0. If 𝑚∈(𝑚−,𝑚+)m∈(m−,m+) there exists a UMP level 𝛼α test in 𝒞𝑚Cm, say 𝜙∗ϕ∗.

𝜙∗ϕ∗ is a two-sided test.

  • It is easy to see that (th, 12.20 Keener (2010)), for the exponential family as above:
    𝛽′𝜙(𝜃)=𝑤′(𝜃)𝐸𝜃(𝑇𝜙)+𝑑(log𝑐(𝜃))𝐸𝜃𝜙βϕ′(θ)=w′(θ)Eθ(Tϕ)+d(log⁡c(θ))Eθϕ

Example Let 𝑋∼𝑒𝑥𝑝𝑜𝑛𝑒𝑛𝑡𝑖𝑎𝑙(𝜃)X∼exponential(θ): 𝑓(𝑥∣𝜃)=𝜃𝑒−𝜃𝑥f(x∣θ)=θe−θx for 𝑥>0x>0.

Consider testing 𝐻0:𝜃=1H0:θ=1 vs 𝐻1:𝜃≠1H1:θ≠1 based on one observation only.

The UMPU test then has the form:

𝜙={10(𝑋<𝑡𝑙)∪(𝑋>𝑡𝑢)𝑡𝑙<𝑋<𝑡𝑢ϕ={1(X<tl)∪(X>tu)0tl<X<tu
where 𝑡𝑢,𝑡𝑙tu,tl must satisfy
𝐸1𝜙=1−∫𝑡𝑢𝑡𝑙𝑒−𝑥𝑑𝑥=1−𝑒−𝑡𝑙−𝑒−𝑡𝑢=𝛼E1ϕ=1−∫tltue−xdx=1−e−tl−e−tu=α
 with the constraint 𝛽′𝜙(𝜃0)=0βϕ′(θ0)=0, that implies
0=𝑤′(1)𝐸1𝜙𝑋+𝑑(log(𝑐(1))𝐸1𝜙=1−∫𝑡𝑢𝑡𝑙𝑥𝑒−𝑥𝑑𝑥+𝐸1𝜙⏞𝛼0=w′(1)E1ϕX+d(log⁡(c(1))E1ϕ=1−∫tltuxe−xdx+E1ϕ⏞α
 Putting the two equations together:
𝑡𝑙𝑒−𝑡𝑙=−(𝑒−𝑡𝑙−1+𝛼)log(𝑒−𝑡𝑙−1+𝛼)tle−tl=−(e−tl−1+α)log⁡(e−tl−1+α)
 The solution has to be found numerically.

Solving for 𝑃(𝑋≤𝑡𝑙)=𝑃(𝑋>𝑡𝑢)=𝛼/2P(X≤tl)=P(X>tu)=α/2 simplifies the computation, but in this case the test is biased, because 𝑚(𝜃0)=𝛽′𝜙(𝜃0)≠0m(θ0)=βϕ′(θ0)≠0

0.0010.1000.050.0010.01090.02080.03070.04060.05050.06040.07030.08020.09010.1

Confidence sets

Duality between tests and confidence sets

Tests and confidence intervals a have a one-2-one correspondence:

Recall that a random set 𝑆(𝑋)S(X) is a 1−𝛼1−α confidence region for a parameter 𝜃θ if

𝑃𝜃(𝜃∈𝑆(𝑋))≥1−𝛼,∀𝜃∈Θ.Pθ(θ∈S(X))≥1−α,∀θ∈Θ.

  • Let 𝜙ϕ be a level 𝛼α binary test with acceptance region 𝐴(𝜃0)A(θ0) for the hypothesis 𝜃=𝜃0θ=θ0.

Then the region 𝑆(𝑋)={𝜃:𝑋∈𝐴(𝜃)}S(X)={θ:X∈A(θ)} is a 1−𝛼1−α confidence region. In fact:

𝑃𝜃(𝜃∈𝑆(𝑋))=𝑃𝜃(𝑋∈𝐴(𝜃))≥1−𝛼Pθ(θ∈S(X))=Pθ(X∈A(θ))≥1−α

  • Conversely, given the 1−𝛼1−α confidence region 𝑆S, the test

    𝜙(𝑋)={10𝜃0∈𝑆(𝑋)𝑐𝜃0∈𝑆(𝑋)ϕ(X)={1θ0∈S(X)c0θ0∈S(X)
    is a level 𝛼α test for 𝜃=𝜃0θ=θ0 against 𝜃≠𝜃0θ≠θ0.

  • Optimality of tests should correspond to optimal confidence interval (in some sense).

  • Consider a confidence set 𝑆(𝑋)S(X) defined as the inverse of a binary two-sided test 𝜙ϕ (we assume for convenience Θ=(𝜃𝑙,𝜃𝑢)Θ=(θl,θu) with max(|𝜃𝑙|,|𝜃𝑢|)=∞max(|θl|,|θu|)=∞ allowed).

  • Let 𝜆(𝐴)λ(A) be the Lebesgue measure of the set 𝐴A. The average length of the confidence interval 𝑆S based on the test 𝜙ϕ is:

    𝐸𝜃𝜆(𝑆(𝑋)∖{𝜃})=𝐸𝜃∫Θ∖{𝜃}1{𝜃0∈𝑆(𝑋)}𝑑𝜃0=∫𝒳∫Θ∖{𝜃}1{𝜃0∈𝑆(𝑋)}𝑑𝜃0𝑑𝑃𝜃(𝑥)=∫Θ∖{𝜃}𝑃𝜃(𝜃0∈𝑆(𝑋))𝑑𝜃0=∫Θ∖{𝜃}𝑃𝜃(𝑋∈𝐴(𝜃0))𝑑𝜃0=∫Θ∖{𝜃}(1−𝛽(𝜃0))𝑑𝜃0Eθλ(S(X)∖{θ})=Eθ∫Θ∖{θ}1{θ0∈S(X)}dθ0=∫X∫Θ∖{θ}1{θ0∈S(X)}dθ0dPθ(x)=∫Θ∖{θ}Pθ(θ0∈S(X))dθ0=∫Θ∖{θ}Pθ(X∈A(θ0))dθ0=∫Θ∖{θ}(1−β(θ0))dθ0
    Since a UMP(U) test 𝜙∗ϕ∗ maximizes 𝛽∗(𝜃0)β∗(θ0) for all 𝜃0≠𝜃θ0≠θ, the mean length of the corresponding confidence set 𝑆∗S∗ is
    𝐸𝜃𝜆(𝑆(𝑋)∖{𝜃})≥𝐸𝜃𝜆(𝑆∗(𝑋)∖{𝜃})Eθλ(S(X)∖{θ})≥Eθλ(S∗(X)∖{θ})

Example Let 𝑋∼𝑒𝑥𝑝𝑜𝑛𝑒𝑛𝑡𝑖𝑎𝑙(𝜆)X∼exponential(λ) (with 𝜆=𝐸𝑋λ=EX). Suppose we want a confidence interval for 𝜆λ.

We can obtain it by inverting a LR test for

𝐻0:𝜆=𝜆0𝑣𝑠𝐻1:𝜆≠𝜆0H0:λ=λ0vsH1:λ≠λ0

Le LRT statistic is:

𝜆(𝑥)=𝜆−10exp{−∑𝑖𝑥𝑖/𝜆0}sup𝜆>0𝜆−1exp{−∑𝑖𝑥𝑖/𝜆}=𝜆−10exp{−∑𝑖𝑥𝑖/𝜆0}𝑥¯−1exp{−∑𝑖𝑥𝑖/𝑥¯}=(𝑥¯𝜆0)𝑛𝑒−𝑛𝑥¯𝜆0+𝑛λ(x)=λ0−1exp⁡{−∑ixi/λ0}supλ>0λ−1exp⁡{−∑ixi/λ}=λ0−1exp⁡{−∑ixi/λ0}x¯−1exp⁡{−∑ixi/x¯}=(x¯λ0)ne−nx¯λ0+n

Rejection region is for small values of 𝜆(𝑥)λ(x)

So, acceptance region is

𝐴(𝜆0)={𝑥:(𝑥¯𝜆0)𝑛𝑒−𝑛(𝑥¯/𝜆0−1)≥𝑘}A(λ0)={x:(x¯λ0)ne−n(x¯/λ0−1)≥k}
where 𝑘k is defined implicitly by 𝑃𝜆0(𝑋∉𝐴(𝜆0))=𝛼.Pλ0(X∉A(λ0))=α.

Then, we obtain the confidence interval:

𝐶𝐼(𝑥)={𝜆:(𝑥¯𝜆)𝑛𝑒−𝑛(𝑥¯/𝜆−1)≥𝑘}={𝜆:(𝑥¯𝜆)𝑛𝑒−𝑛𝑥¯/𝜆≥𝑘𝑒−𝑛}CI(x)={λ:(x¯λ)ne−n(x¯/λ−1)≥k}={λ:(x¯λ)ne−nx¯/λ≥ke−n}

Pivotal Random Variables

Def.: A random variable 𝑄(𝑋,𝜃)Q(X,θ) is a pivotal quantity (or pivot) if its distribution does not depend on the parameter 𝜃θ. Here 𝑋X is the random sample.

  • If the pivotal quantity can be inverted as a function of 𝜃θ for each value of 𝑋X , then we may find a confidence region (interval) for 𝜃θ.
  • Inversion means that, for each 𝑥x and each subset 𝐴A of the range of 𝑄(𝑥,𝜃)Q(x,θ), there exists a subset 𝑅(𝑥,𝐴)R(x,A) of ΘΘ such that
    𝑄(𝑥,𝜃)∈𝐴⟺𝜃∈𝑅(𝑥,𝐴)Q(x,θ)∈A⟺θ∈R(x,A)
     This implies that
    𝑃(𝑄(𝑋,𝜃)∈𝐴)=𝑃(𝜃∈𝑅(𝑋,𝐴))P(Q(X,θ)∈A)=P(θ∈R(X,A))
  • From a (nontrivial) pivotal random variable one can often obtain a confidence interval
  • In general there may be more than one pivotal random variable and confidence intervals

Minimum lenght confidence intervals

An optimality theorem for confidence intervals is available for unimodal distributions.

Theorem(9.3.2 Casella and Berger (2024)) Assume 𝑇=𝑇(𝑋)T=T(X) is a pivotal statistic for 𝜃θ and that its distribution 𝑓(𝑡)f(t) is unimodal. If in the interval [𝑎,𝑏][a,b] satisfies

  1. ∫𝑏𝑎𝑓(𝑡)𝑑𝑡=1−𝛼∫abf(t)dt=1−α

  2. 𝑓(𝑎)=𝑓(𝑏)>0f(a)=f(b)>0

  3. 𝑎≤𝑡⋆≤𝑏a≤t⋆≤b where 𝑡⋆t⋆ is a mode for 𝑓(𝑡)f(t)

Then [𝑎,𝑏][a,b] is the shortest among all intervals that satisfies ∫𝑏𝑎𝑓(𝑡)𝑑𝑡=1−𝛼∫abf(t)dt=1−α.

Example (continued)

10
00.0499000.02500.0050.010.0150.020.0250.030.0350.040.0450.0499

Fisher vs Neyman

  • The approach outlined so far follows Neyman-Pearson hypothesis testing framework: the problem of testing is treated as a decision problem.

  • Neyman-Pearson wanted to find a mathematical justification to the significance tests of the Fisher school.

  • Even so, Fisher was quite adamant in opposing their approach.

  • Fisher refused the idea of the optimality as a property of a procedure, and claimed the central role of the experience of the research in making an informed decision conditional on the problem
  • Therefore, maximization of power is not relevant. Fisher is not very much interested in the alternative: a large value of the test statistic gives evidence against the null, because it makes less plausible the null hypothesis (low probability to observe that value under the null)

  • The approach outlined so far follows Neyman-Pearson hypothesis testing framework: the problem of testing is treated as a decision problem.

  • Neyman-Pearson wanted to find a mathematical justification to the significance tests of the Fisher school.

  • Even so, Fisher was quite adamant in opposing their approach.

  • While for N-P, the choice based on optimality is pre-experimental, for Fisher every conclusion (significance level) must be conditional on an available distribution constant. N-P principle is coherent with the idea of repetibility of the experiment

  • Sometimes the contrast is also referred as c contrast between p-values (or significance) and pre-determined 𝛼α level.

P-variables and p-values

Let us consider the null hypothesis Θ0Θ0.

Def: A 𝑝−p−variable for Θ0Θ0 is a [0,1]−[0,1]−valued random variable 𝑈U such that, for every 𝛼∈(0,1)α∈(0,1), and for all 𝜃∈Θ0θ∈Θ0:

𝑃𝜃(𝑈≤𝛼)≤𝛼Pθ(U≤α)≤α
The 𝑝−p−variable is exact if 𝑃𝜃(𝑈≤𝛼)=𝛼Pθ(U≤α)=α.

Def: A 𝑝−p−value is the value taken by the 𝑝−p−variable on observing the data.

A test that rejects 𝐻0:𝜃∈Θ0H0:θ∈Θ0 if and only if 𝑈=𝑈(𝑋)≤𝛼U=U(X)≤α is a level 𝛼α test by construction.

A test result can be reported via its 𝑝−p−value rather than the acceptance/rejection decision. This clearly gives more information, because it reports the result of the test on a continuous scale.

Theorem Let 𝜙ϕ be a one-sided test with rejection region 𝑅(𝜃)={𝑇(𝑋)≥𝑘}R(θ)={T(X)≥k}. Define for each sample point 𝑥x, the function

𝑝(𝑥)=sup𝜃∈Θ0𝑃𝜃(𝑇(𝑋)≥𝑇(𝑥))p(x)=supθ∈Θ0Pθ(T(X)≥T(x))
Then, the r.v. 𝑝(𝑋)p(X) is a 𝑝−p−variable.

Proof. For any 𝜃0∈Θ0θ0∈Θ0

𝑝𝜃0(𝑥)=𝑃𝜃0(𝑇(𝑋)≥𝑇(𝑥))=𝑃𝜃0(−𝑇(𝑋)≤−𝑇(𝑥))=𝐹𝜃0(−𝑇(𝑥))pθ0(x)=Pθ0(T(X)≥T(x))=Pθ0(−T(X)≤−T(x))=Fθ0(−T(x))
where 𝐹𝜃0Fθ0 is the CDF of −𝑇(𝑋)−T(X) under 𝜃0θ0. Then, by the probability integral transform, 𝐹𝜃0(−𝑇(𝑋))=𝑑𝑝𝜃0(𝑋)∼𝑈(0,1)Fθ0(−T(X))=dpθ0(X)∼U(0,1).

Thus, for any 𝛼∈(0,1)α∈(0,1), 𝑃𝜃(𝑝(𝑋)≤𝛼)=𝑃𝜃(sup𝜃𝑝𝜃(𝑋)≤𝛼)≤𝑃𝜃(𝑝𝜃(𝑋)≤𝛼)=𝛼Pθ(p(X)≤α)=Pθ(supθpθ(X)≤α)≤Pθ(pθ(X)≤α)=α

References

Casella, G., and R. Berger. 2024. Statistical Inference. Chapman & Hall.
Keener, Robert W. 2010. Theoretical Statistics. Topics for a Core Course. Springer.
Lehmann, E. L. 1986. Testing Statistical Hypotheses. Springer Texts in Statistics. Springer.
Lehmann, E. L., and Joseph P. Romano. 2022. Testing Statistical Hypotheses. Springer. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-70578-7.
